title: Namespaces, Metadata, Labels, Selectors, Ownership, and Deletion · Independent Platform Trial
summary: An independent delivery team receives an unfamiliar namespace, failure domain, and policy constraint and must transfer namespaces, metadata, labels, selectors, ownership, and deletion into a new resource identity and lifecycle policy. Planned artifact: Produce an independent changed-context version of resource identity and lifecycle policy with automated and judgment evidence. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defense.
objectives: Distinguish namespaced and cluster-scoped resources, default namespaces, cross-namespace references, quotas, and isolation limits.
Design stable recommended labels and equality or set selectors without accidental overlap, mutable identity, or controller mismatch.
Separate identifying labels from non-identifying annotations and preserve controller, tooling, audit, and rollout metadata ownership.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

starterFiles.config:
# kubernetes-basics cumulative orchestration portfolio
# Browser labs perform deterministic manifest and evidence review only.
# They never invoke kubectl, read kubeconfig, contact a cluster API, or execute host commands.
workspace: kubernetes
version-baseline:
  kubernetes: 1.36.2
  minikube: 1.38.1
  gateway-api: 1.5.1
  helm: 4.2.2
transfer-boundary: authorized-disposable-cluster
evidence:

steps.title: Commit to the mission model · Lab K8s Namespace Scope Boundaries
steps.instruction:
lab case — A lab namespace contains similarly named objects, copied labels, a dangling owner reference, and a finalizer blocking deletion. Design names, labels, selectors, annotations, ownership, garbage collection, quota boundaries, and deletion evidence.

What must be established before implementation?

Evidence target: Commit to the mission model · Lab K8s Namespace Scope Boundaries.
steps.why: The decision supplies observable evidence for distinguish namespaced and cluster-scoped resources, default namespaces, cross-namespace references, quotas, and isolation limits. For Commit to the mission model · Lab K8s Namespace Scope Boundaries, keep K8s Namespace Scope Boundaries connected to mechanically reviewable Kubernetes object, status, and transfer evidence.

steps.title: Extend the cumulative artifact · Lab K8s Finalizers Deletion Recovery
steps.instruction:
Append the Kubernetes evidence item headed "  # Evidence: k8s-finalizers-deletion-recovery-1dci1j". Tailor its metadata-policy.yaml change to the current stakeholder case, retain a deterministic API or manifest review command, reject one concrete failure, and record a repair. The browser analyzes text only; run kubectl, Helm, kubeadm, and cluster commands later in an authorized disposable transfer environment.

Evidence target: Extend the cumulative artifact · Lab K8s Finalizers Deletion Recovery.
steps.why: Building a runnable or mechanically reviewable artifact turns K8s Finalizers Deletion Recovery into observable performance evidence. For Extend the cumulative artifact · Lab K8s Finalizers Deletion Recovery, keep K8s Finalizers Deletion Recovery connected to mechanically reviewable Kubernetes object, status, and transfer evidence.

content.code:
# Evidence: k8s-finalizers-deletion-recovery-1dci1j
  - competency: k8s-finalizers-deletion-recovery
    artifact: metadata-policy.yaml
    decision: finalizers deletion recovery
    environment: clean conformance cluster with only required add-ons installed
    fault-injection: remove DNS egress from an otherwise ready workload
    retained-proof: authorization review token audience and audit correlation
    case-key: 1dci1j
    change: |
      namespace: intake
      labels: app.kubernetes.io/name=status-api
      selector-owner: deployment-status-api
      owner-uid: retained
      deletion: foreground
      finalizer-controller: archive-cleanup
    verify: kubectl get all -n intake --show-labels -o json && kubectl get namespace intake -o json
    failure: Reject namespace-as-sandbox claims, overlapping selectors, name-only owner matching, or blind finalizer removal.
    repair: Separate scope and security, stabilize identity labels, trace owner UIDs and dependents, and identify external cleanup before deletion.
content.caption: Shape of the required increment; replace generic language with the current stakeholder case.

content.title: Evidence is more than syntax
content.text: The K8s Finalizers Deletion Recovery structural check is only one gate. resource identity and lifecycle policy must also survive the stated changed case and causal explanation.

lines.text: A changed authentic task independently demonstrates this outcome: Interpret deletionTimestamp and finalizers, identify the responsible controller, preserve external cleanup evidence, and repair stuck deletion safely.

options.text: Reject the unsupported conclusion, name the violated K8s Finalizers Deletion Recovery condition, repair the responsible layer, and rerun the boundary trace.

Keep the plausible K8s Finalizers Deletion Recovery output and hide the violated resource identity and lifecycle policy assumption instead of explaining the failure. That claim fails the test an acceptance boundary evidence boundary for K8s Finalizers Deletion Recovery.

For test an acceptance boundary, copy one familiar manifest or kubectl command, omit context, API discovery, desired-versus-observed status, failure, rollback, and disposable-cluster transfer checks, then accept an Applied response as production proof. steps.title: Defend the transferred model · Lab K8s Finalizers Deletion Recovery
steps.instruction:
Write the lab evidence note for K8s Finalizers Deletion Recovery: explain the governing condition, one failure, and how this concrete case was verified — A lab namespace contains similarly named objects, copied labels, a dangling owner reference, and a finalizer blocking deletion. Design names, labels, selectors, annotations, ownership, garbage collection, quota boundaries, and deletion evidence. Competency probe: Interpret deletionTimestamp and finalizers, identify the responsible controller, preserve external cleanup evidence, and repair stuck deletion safely.

Evidence target: Defend the transferred model · Lab K8s Finalizers Deletion Recovery.
steps.why: Retrieving K8s Finalizers Deletion Recovery through the concrete resource identity and lifecycle policy case exposes causal gaps that recognition alone can hide. For Defend the transferred model · Lab K8s Finalizers Deletion Recovery, keep K8s Finalizers Deletion Recovery connected to mechanically reviewable Kubernetes object, status, and transfer evidence.

content.title: Evidence defense
content.text: Use the case, one assumption, one observable result, and one limitation. Do not copy the competency statement: Interpret deletionTimestamp and finalizers, identify the responsible controller, preserve external cleanup evidence, and repair stuck deletion safely.

hints: For Defend the transferred model · Lab K8s Finalizers Deletion Recovery, name the context, namespace, API identity, desired state, observed status, controller, policy, and failure-domain boundary, K8s Finalizers Deletion Recovery precondition, or invariant that must hold.

At Defend the transferred model · Lab K8s Finalizers Deletion Recovery, trace this concrete input and predict admission, defaulting, scheduling, reconciliation, status, traffic, storage, policy, disruption, and cleanup evidence before reviewing it: A lab namespace contains similarly named objects, copied labels, a dangling owner reference, and a finalizer blocking deletion. Design names, labels, selectors, annotations, ownership, garbage collection, quota boundaries, and deletion evidence. Competency probe: Interpret deletionTimestamp and finalizers, identify the responsible controller, preserve external cleanup evidence, and repair stuck deletion safely.

For Defend the transferred model · Lab K8s Finalizers Deletion Recovery, challenge the documented misconception with object, condition, event, policy, failure, repair, rollback, and transfer evidence: Deleting finalizers immediately is the normal first repair for any terminating resource.
